So eating honey is not helping (pollinating) insects. Instead, to increase biodiversity you can eat more organic products and keep native plants in your garden.

pages.subtitle: This recipe includes apples, a product that’s easy to buy organically and support more biodiversity.

pages.hometitle: Can bee hotels help to increase the number of wild bees?
pages.title: There are many different types of pollinators but bees are the most important group 🐝
Can bee hotels help to increase the number of wild bees? 🏨
The majority of bee species live…

answerExplanation.text:
3/4 of all bee species live underground, which means bee hotels are not the perfect home for most bees.

Still, research shows that they can help, particularly as the bee hotels get older. There were more pollinators in bee hotels that were older and when they had more flowers nearby.

But placing a few bee hotels in an area full of pavement is not going to do the job.

dailyContent.notificationText: Can you get ‘complete’ protein from plants? 🥦 - Recipe of the day: Caribbean Chilli

home.title: Can you get ‘complete’ protein from plants?

pages.title: Protein consists of 20 different amino acids. We need the right mix to get ‘complete’ protein 💪
Many people believe that only meat contains all essentail amino acids. This is false ❌ All foods contain all amino acids, but to different levels.
So the question is: how do we make sure we get the right mix?
What is most important to get the right mix of amino acids?

answerOptions.title: Only eating certain foods in the morning
Paying attention to food combinations in one meal
Eating diverse foods during the day

answerExplanation.text:
It is false that you can only get all 20 amino acids from meat. It’s also not true that you need to eat the right combinations in one meal (e.g. rice and beans). All you need is to eat a diverse range of foods within 24 hours.

In other words, there is no need to worry about ‘complete’ protein.

pages.subtitle: One of the easiest ways to eat diverse food is making it more colorful!

pages.hometitle: Can you get enough protein from plants if you do a LOT of sports?
pages.title: Okay, so you can get ‘complete’ protein with only plants. But can you also get enough protein from plants if you do a LOT of sports? 🏋️‍♂️
The general recommended amount of protein is 0.83 g per kg bodyweight ⚖️
For what amount of the population of healthy adults should this be enough? 💪

answerExplanation.text:
The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) concludes that 0.83 g of protein per kg bodyweight is enough for virtually everyone (97.5% of healthy adults). Many people think it should be higher but the EFSA advises that the extra burnt calories simply require more food, not a focus on extra protein.

The only issue when you do a LOT of sports, like professional athletes, is that you cannot eat and digest that much, so then special solutions come in. Also good to know: they didn’t find any extra benefit for either muscle mass or performance when increasing protein intake.

dailyContent.notificationText: How Fairtrade works 👨🏾‍🌾 - Recipe of the day: Banana Curry

home.title: How Fairtrade works

pages.title: We’ve all heard about Fairtrade products, but what do the farmers actually get? 👨🏾‍🌾
How does Fairtrade work? 💸

answerOptions.title: Farmers are guaranteed a minimum price
Farmers get a premium on top of their sales price

answerExplanation.text:
Many farmers around the world are small and don’t have negotiation power with big companies. That’s why the Fairtrade minimum price is an important structure.

Fairtrade also includes a premium. It’s an extra sum of money that goes into a fund. Farmers and workers can decide where it's spend and they often use it for housing, education, or improving the farm.

pages.subtitle: Bananas are an easy product to buy Fairtrade.

pages.hometitle: Do Fairtrade farmers and workers receive a ‘living wage’?
pages.title: Every person should receive a ‘living wage’: an amount of money that’s enough to have decent standard of living in their country 💸
Fairtrade farmers and workers receive a ‘living wage’ 👨🏽‍🌾

answerExplanation.text:
Even with Fairtrade, farmers and workers do not automatically receive a living wage. This is a quote from Fairtrade itself: “Current wages are so far below living wages, that individual plantations and farmers have no way to achieve a living wage for workers and remain competitive in the market.”

So we would say Fairtrade is more like ‘minimum trade’. It’s better than nothing, but still falls short. Let’s not fool ourselves: many of our products come from modern slavery and more responsible food choices aren’t going to fix it. We need better laws and a different food system.

dailyContent.notificationText: Does putting a lid on the pan save energy? 🔥 - Recipe of the day: Spicy Green Penne

home.title: Does putting a lid on the pan save energy?

pages.title: We already covered that the energy of cooking has a much lower climate impact than the ingredients 🍳
But there are a lot of opinions about how to reduce energy use while cooking. So let’s take a closer look 🔎
What saves the most energy when cooking pasta?

answerOptions.title: Turning heat down after water boils and pasta is added

answerExplanation.text:
Putting the lid on traps the heat but doesn’t reduce the energy that goes into the pan. The most effective thing is to actually reduce the amount of energy.

If you’re a climate nerd you now know how to reduce cooking energy. But don’t stress too much about it: the ingredients that go into the pan are more important.

pages.subtitle: Use your new pasta knowledge with this simple and delicious recipe.

pages.hometitle: What are effective ways to reduce your gas usage?
pages.title: There are many ways to reduce the amount of gas at home 🔥
What makes the SMALLEST difference?
pages.subtitle: for saving gas

answerExplanation.text:
Turning the heating down or taking shorter hot showers can make a real difference. Switching to induction doesn’t save as much gas but it does help to reduce indoor air pollution.

What we find interesting about this: habit change makes a bigger difference (for the climate and your wallet) than buying new stuff!

dailyContent.notificationText: Sea vegetables 🌊 - Recipe of the day: Mushroom Ginger Noodle Soup

home.title: Sea vegetables

pages.title: The majority of our food comes from land but we also get some food from the Ocean. Mostly by hunting or farming fish 🐟
We already saw that farming mussels is much more sustainable but there is another way to farm the oceans: seaweed! 🌊
What is a benefit of seaweed?

answerExplanation.text:
The biggest benefit of seaweed is that we can grow vegetables without any land, fertilizer, or irrigation. Farming seaweed is also easy and cheap and most varieties are very nutritious.

Seaweed might not be a common product, but if you can find it, give this climate superfood a try!

pages.subtitle: You could add some sheets of Nori seaweed to this dish (often used for sushi).

pages.hometitle: How can seaweed capture CO2 like a forest?
pages.title: A challenge for seaweed is making it popular enough as food 📣 But there are other options for seaweed.
One option is to use it as an alternative to planting trees to capture CO2 🌳 But can seaweed grow fast enough?
How fast does seaweed grow?
pages.subtitle: in the case of kelp in ideal conditions

answerExplanation.text:
Planting seaweed is a great alternative to planting trees, because - again - it requires no land and fertilizer. And it can grow very fast!

Planting more seaweed is one of the climate solutions in Project Drawdown. Another one is protecting existing seaweed areas, which are currently shrinking because of human activity.

dailyContent.notificationText: Can you afford Fairtrade & organic products? 💰 - Recipe of the day: Creamy Tomato Pasta with Hazelnuts

home.title: Can you afford Fairtrade & organic products?

pages.title: In rich countries people spend around 8-10% of their income on food. In poor countries it’s up to 50% 💰
How much did people spend on food in 1960 compared to today?
pages.subtitle: Data from the Netherlands, share of total

answerExplanation.text:
In rich countries we spend less and less on basic needs and use more of our money on luxuries. This also has a climate cost: the richer you are the higher your footprint (on average).

At the same time, many farmers are not getting paid enough. So we think it’s important to be aware of this decline in food spending. Maybe we can afford to buy more organic and Fairtrade products than we think.

pages.subtitle: Organic pasta is often not that much more expensive.

pages.hometitle: What is the cheapest way to reduce your carbon footprint?
pages.title: Some solutions like fairtrade & organic food cost money 💶 And while those are worth it, it’s good to remember that there are many solutions that don’t cost extra.
A study in Sweden found that the average person can reduce their footprint by 40%, without spending extra money! 🤑
Which category saved the most CO2?
pages.subtitle: both in absolute and relative terms

answerExplanation.text:
In the study they replaced flying and car travel with a train trip and a staycation with activities like concerts and a massage. All of that would cost the same but cut emissions by up to 90%.

With eating more plant-based, emissions were also cut by ca. 1/3 without spending extra. So even if you can’t afford Fairtrade and organic, your actions still make a big difference!

dailyContent.notificationText: The most produced crop in the world 🥇 - Recipe of the day: Chilli Spaghetti

home.title: The most produced crop in the world

pages.title: The most produced crop in the world is not wheat, corn, or rice. It’s sugarcane! 🥇
It’s used for many different things, like packaging, fuel, and of course sugar. How does the climate impact of sugar compare to other foods?
Which ingredient in a chocolate cake has the highest climate impact?

answerExplanation.text: The footprint per kg of sugar is not very high. The biggest reason to be careful with sugar is still your own health.

pages.subtitle: Sometimes a little bit of sugar can make a tomato sauce more balanced. Try it with this delicious Chilli spaghetti.

pages.hometitle: How much sugar do we eat per day?
pages.title: On average, people in the Netherlands think they eat 7 cubes of sugar per day 💭
How many sugar cubes do they actually eat? 🍬
pages.subtitle: in the Netherlands per person per day

answerExplanation.text: People in the Netherlands eat twice as much sugar as they think! An important reason for this is that sugar is added to many products like spice mixes. Sugar also has 50 different names, like dextrose, which makes it more difficult to recognize.
answerExplanation.sourceURL: https://www.diabetesfonds.nl/speciaal-voor/voor-de-pers/nederlanders-consumeren-meer-suiker-dan-ze-zelf-denken#:~:text=Nederlanders consumeren twee keer zoveel,is dit ruim 14 suikerklontjes.

dailyContent.notificationText: How sustainable is a BBQ? 🔥 - Recipe of the day: Low Impact BBQ

home.title: How sustainable is a BBQ?

pages.title: At Fork Ranger we love a good campfire 🔥 But burning wood also causes CO2 and pollution.
The same is true for a barbecue: what’s the impact of grilling with charcoal?
What has a higher climate impact?

answerOptions.title: Charcoal for one BBQ session (1.5 kg) 🔥

answerExplanation.text:
A barbecue burns wood (or fossil fuel) and is not really sustainable in the era of climate change, but it’s a luxury we can allow ourselves once in a while. Much more important is what we put on the BBQ.

Another important note: buy charcoal with the FSC mark for sustainable forestry.

pages.subtitle: Try some of these veggie options at your next BBQ!

pages.hometitle: Are meat alternatives a good option for BBQ?
pages.title: How acceptable people find meat alternatives can depend on the situation 😶
For example, a study found that meat alternatives feel more acceptable when eating alone than when they are invited to a restaurant 👥
The acceptance of meat alternatives at a BBQ party is… 🍔
pages.subtitle: compared to other dinner settings

answerExplanation.text:
Both meat eaters and flexitarians find meat alternatives less acceptable at a BBQ party than at other situations like a dinner with friends. Another good reason to put a lot of vegetables on the grill.

When it comes to hosting vegetarians and vegans at a BBQ, it often happens that the host buys meat alternatives that the guests don’t even enjoy. One effective suggestion from a study about this topic: ask the guests to bring their own favorites. This way the vegetarians will be happy and meat eaters can try some new things as well!

dailyContent.notificationText: What is regenerative agriculture? 🧑‍🌾 - Recipe of the day: Bean Soup with Salsa

home.title: What is regenerative agriculture?

pages.title: Regenerative agriculture describes a way of farming that *improves* nature, rather than destroying it 🌈
But there is no official definition 🏭 So people fear that big companies will use it for greenwashing.
One study looked at how most research and websites define regenerative agriculture 🔎 There is one outcome that was mentioned most often.
What is the most mentioned outcome of regenerative agriculture?

answerOptions.title: Taking up more CO2 in the soil ☁️

answerExplanation.text:
At the core of regenerative agriculture is improving the soil. This is often achieved by avoiding ‘inputs’ like chemical fertilizer or pesticides, making farm animals part of the system, and not tilling the land.

The easiest way to support regenerative agriculture is buying organic products, because there is a lot of overlap between these two concepts.

pages.subtitle: A delicious bean recipe. And as you know by now: beans improve the soil.

pages.hometitle: What is the main practice of regenerative agriculture?
pages.title: The biggest focus is improving soil health, but how do farmers do this? 🧑‍🌾
The same study also asked that question and analyzed how often certain activities are mentioned 🔍
Which activity is mentioned most often?
pages.subtitle: to define regenerative agriculture

answerExplanation.text:
Regenerative farms use all of these but the most mentioned activity is minimizing ‘external inputs’ like fertilizer and pesticides.

The second most-mentioned activity was combining crops and animals on the same farm. This is in contrast to the 80% of farms in the EU that are specialized in either crops or livestock.

dailyContent.notificationText: How many people actually care about the climate? 🧐 - Recipe of the day: Lasagne with Walnuts

home.title: How many people actually care about the climate?

pages.title: For a study in the US, people were asked: How many people do you think are worried about climate change? Their answer: 43%.
Then they looked at how many people actually care 🧐
How many people are actually worried about climate change?
pages.subtitle: in the US

answerExplanation.text: Two-thirds of all Americans worry about climate change. But people *think* that this number is only 43%! So speak up, there are lots of people who care about the climate but stay silent. We need to support each other 💚

pages.subtitle: Use this lasagne to invite friends for dinner and a talk about climate change.

pages.hometitle: How many people around the world agree that climate change is a serious threat?
pages.title: A different study surveyed 59,000 people in 63 countries and asked ‘Do you believe in climate change as a serious threat to humanity’? ⚠️
In the country with the lowest score, how many people agreed that climate change is a serious threat? ⬇️

answerExplanation.text:
Even in the countries with the lowest agreement, 73% agree that climate change is a problem.

The majority in every single country also supported policies like a carbon tax, expanding public transport, more renewable energy, more electric car chargers, taxes on airlines, and protecting forests.

dailyContent.notificationText: The cost of Fairtrade 💸 - Recipe of the day: Spicy Greens with Peanut Sauce

home.title: The cost of Fairtrade

pages.title: When you see Fairtrade bananas right next to the cheaper bananas, it can feel like a tough choice 🍌
Each trip to the supermarket you feel this dilemma: good for my wallet or good for the people? 🤔
But what if you make your choice for a whole year?
How much extra would it cost to buy 3 Fairtrade bananas a week, for a whole year? 🍌
pages.subtitle: in the Netherlands

answerExplanation.text:
How do you feel about this difference? For Mareike from the Fork Ranger team this was less than she thought. And therefore she decided to buy Fairtrade bananas from now onwards.

In an ideal world, supermarkets would pay a fair price for ALL bananas. But for now, buying the Fairtrade option not only ensures a fair wage but it’s also an investment into a different food system.

pages.subtitle: You can start by making the choice to always buy the Fairtrade version of one product, like bananas, coffee, or in this case: rice.

pages.hometitle: How much would the price of meat increase with ‘true pricing’?
pages.title: A ‘true price’ of a product not only includes the financial costs but also the societal costs that are caused by environmental damage 💰
A German study found that the true price of non-organic crops would on average get 0,79€ more expensive per kg 🌾
How much would the price of meat increase? 🍗
pages.subtitle: average per kg for beef, pork, chicken

answerExplanation.text:
All products - whether organic or not - get more expensive if you include the environmental damage. But the price of organic products is closer to the true price.

For vegetables, the price doesn’t increase that much but meat would be twice as expensive with a true price! The biggest price increase happens with beef, which would be 3x as expensive as current prices.

dailyContent.notificationText: What are the benefits of feeding cows with grass only? - Recipe of the day: Roasted Beets and Carrots

home.title: What are the benefits of feeding cows with grass only?

pages.title: Feeding cows grass instead of corn is better for the cows 🐄 But we’ve already covered that it’s not automatically better for the climate. What about biodiversity?
There are lots of people with strong opinions about grass-fed cows but there is very little scientific research 🤨
We found a paper that reviewed the impact of grass-fed dairy cows on biodiversity 🐝
Which description of a product is more likely to support greater biodiversity?
pages.subtitle: for dairy products

answerExplanation.text:
The problem with grass-fed products is that it can still come from grass monocultures sprayed with chemical fertilizer.

Organic is not only about avoiding pesticides and chemical fertilizer, but also has lots of other requirements, like that animals must be outside part of the time. All of this means organic meat and dairy are better for biodiversity. But only if we also reduce the amounts we eat.

Of course when it comes to increasing biodiversity, there is also the option to have no cows at all.

pages.subtitle: We can also eat grass ourselves, or at least the seeds. Wheat is a type of grass and this recipe includes wheat in the form of bulgur.

pages.hometitle: How much soy and corn do organic cows eat?
pages.title: Dairy cows not only eat grass but also corn and ‘concentrate’ like soy 🌽 A Spanish study compared the diets of different farming methods.
How much concentrate and corn do organic cows eat? 🐄
pages.subtitle: compared to conventional cows

answerExplanation.text:
Organic cows also eat maize and power food, but at lower levels. The irony is that the Spanish study describes this as a problem because the organic cows give less milk.

This illustrates that only switching to organic meat & dairy is not the solution. If we switch to organic we need more cows and more land.

That's why we need to eat less of these products. But for the small amounts we do eat, it makes sense to have them organic.

dailyContent.notificationText: The climate impact of your oven 🔥 - Recipe of the day: Roasted Brussel Sprouts and Cauliflower

home.title: The climate impact of your oven

pages.title: We’ve already covered the energy use of induction vs. gas. But what about the oven? ♨️
Let‘s say you‘re roasting potatoes for 30 minutes instead of frying them in the pan 🥔
How much higher is the climate impact of the oven?
pages.subtitle: compared to frying potatoes in the pan

answerExplanation.text: Maybe we shouldn’t use the oven every day but clearly it’s more important to focus on the impact of the ingredients. Especially since in a few years, all electricity will be renewable 😎

pages.subtitle: Roasting veggies in the oven is one of the easiest ways to create a lot of flavor.

pages.hometitle: What’s the most effective way to reduce the energy use of the oven?
pages.title: The oven uses a lot of energy but are there ways we can reduce it? ⚡️
Which of these is the best strategy to save energy? ⚡️

answerOptions.title: Turning it off 5-10 minutes before the end

answerExplanation.text:
You can turn the oven off 5-10 minutes before the end of the cooking time and let the food finish in the hot oven. This works well for our recipes but don’t use it for baking because that requires more precision.

Pre-heating the oven can be important for the flavor, as the immediate heat creates a different effect than gradual warming. For example, pre-heating is necessary to get crispy potatoes.

dailyContent.notificationText: Is hunting sustainable? 🏹 - Recipe of the day: Spinach Potato Curry

home.title: Is hunting sustainable?

pages.title: We’ve learned a lot about farm animals. It’s time to look at wild animals 🐗
Let’s explore two areas in the Netherlands with deer: in one of them, no hunting is allowed 🦌
And in the other area, they shoot deer so there aren’t too many of them 🏹
What happened to the area where they shot deer?

answerExplanation.text:
A lot of our nature is out of balance: there are no predators and because of streets and cities, deer can’t migrate to other areas. Then they crowd out other animals and plants.

Shooting them is one solution, and the majority of hunting in the Netherlands happens for this reason. So this can be a form of sustainable meat, but there is not enough to replace our current meat consumption.

Also when it comes to wild meat, keep in mind that the situation (and therefore sustainability) differs a lot from region to region and for different animal types. This is just one example.

pages.subtitle: To be honest, this curry has nothing to do with hunting. But it’s easy and delicious.

pages.hometitle: Is wild meat really wild?
pages.title: Some of the meat that we think of as ‘wild’ is actually farmed 🚜
Which of these is always wild?
pages.subtitle: in the Netherlands

answerExplanation.text:
In the Netherlands, meat from duck and rabbit is often farmed. And when it comes to deer it depends on the species.

Fallow deer have white spots on them and often come from a farm (you also see them in city parks). Roe deer are smaller and browner. They don’t eat grass and are considered more nervous and solitary. That's why you can't farm them, and it's always wild meat if you see it on a menu.

dailyContent.notificationText: The impact of cultivated meat 🍖 - Recipe of the day: Balsamic Broccoli Noodles

home.title: The impact of cultivated meat

pages.title: Besides making meat plant-based, there are also initiatives to grow meat without animals 🧫
It works by taking cells from an animal and then growing those into meat 🍖
Right now cultivated meat is still very expensive 💰 And the carbon footprint ranges from very low to much higher than beef.
What determines the carbon footprint of cultivated meat?

answerOptions.title: Type of animal the cells come from 🐄
How much of the energy is renewable ⚡️

answerExplanation.text:
Cultivated meat is not yet widely available, apart from a handful of high-end restaurants. So a study estimated the carbon footprint of cultivated meat in 2030.

The impact was determined by the energy mix: when all energy is renewable, the impact is expected to be comparable to plant-based meat. But with the expected energy mix of 2030, the impact is higher than pork and chicken.

pages.subtitle: This recipe includes tofu, which has roughly the same footprint as the best-case scenario for cultivated meat.

pages.hometitle: Does cultivated meat taste exactly the same?
pages.title: In 2022, an Israeli startup served a piece of cultivated chicken to professional taster Michal Ansky 🍗
They did a blind tasting with real chicken and ground up both versions so there wouldn’t be a difference in texture.
Did she taste a difference? 👅

answerExplanation.text: It was a blind tasting and she was convinced that the cultivated chicken was the real one. This story shows the potential of the solution. But for now, there remain a lot of challenges around costs and scaling up production.

dailyContent.notificationText: What’s the most sustainable coffee? ☕️ - Recipe of the day: Chickpea and Lemon Wraps

home.title: What’s the most sustainable coffee?

pages.title: There are lots of different ways to prepare a cup of coffee ☕️
They all have a unique taste, but do they also differ in their carbon footprint?
Which method of making coffee has the highest footprint? ☕️

answerExplanation.text:
An espresso has the lowest footprint but it’s also just 40 ml. French press also has a low footprint because it doesn’t have use much electricy or create waste (it also contains the most caffeine).

More important than how you make your coffee is the coffee you buy: the espresso would have a much higher impact if we use the average coffee numbers from a big study that reviewed multiple coffees. The most sustainable coffee comes from agroforestry (see the infographic in ‘Fading Forest’).

pages.subtitle: Agroforestry is about mixing agriculture with trees. This recipe includes walnuts, which of course comes from trees and can also be grown in agroforestry systems.

pages.hometitle: Is a reusable cup more sustainable than paper cups?
pages.title: Coffee-to-go comes in paper cups. But it’s not really paper because the inside is lined with plastic 🥤
Let’s say you buy one coffee to-go every day and you switch to a reusable cup ☕️
How long would it take to save emissions? ⏱

answerExplanation.text:
After around 20-40 times a reusable cup saves more CO2 than getting paper cups. So if you’re someone who drinks a lot of coffee on the go, this is a good investment.

But if you only buy a coffee-to-go a few times a year you don’t have to stress about it too much.

home.title: How much does our food contribute to climate change?

pages.title: How much does our food contribute to climate change?
pages.subtitle: yearly greenhouse gas emissions worldwide

answerExplanation.text:
Our global food system causes one third of all emissions. 

On your Fork Ranger journey you’re going to explore the most important sources of these emissions and what we can do about it.

pages.subtitle: This was the first Fork Ranger recipe and it has become a favorite of dozens of people.

home.title: What can save more CO2 emissions?

pages.title: What can save more CO2 emissions?

answerExplanation.text:
The most effective way to reduce the footprint of our food is eating less meat and dairy.

You don’t have to become a vegetarian to be part of the solution. A few simple changes can make a massive difference.

pages.subtitle: Most of us need to eat more veggies and this recipe makes that a lot easier.

home.title: The most effective solutions for climate change

pages.title: Which solution can save the most CO2?

answerExplanation.text:
At Fork Ranger, we are inspired by the fact that there are some very human solutions that can save a lot more CO2 than technology.

It’s all about finding a new way of living that is in harmony with nature:
- less meat, more plants
- less flying, more local adventures
- less stuff, more memories

pages.subtitle: One of our most simple recipes. So it’s to also set a small step on busy days.
